3. (a) (Long. ofM.C. +Long. of Sun) divided by 2; (b) (2 X
long. ofM.C.) +Long. of Sun divided by 3; (c) M.C. + (2 X Long.
of Sun) divided by 3 =Moon, Ascdt. or Descdt.
4. (a) (Long. of Ascdt. +Long. of Sun) divided by 2; (b) (2 X
Ascdt) + Long. of Sun divided by 3; (c) Ascdt. + (2 X Long. of
Sun) divided by 3 = Moon, M.C. or I.C.
5. (a) (M.C. +Long. of Moon) divided by 2; (b) (2 X M.C) -t ·
Long. of Moon divided by 3; (c) M.C. + (2 X Long. of Moon)
divided by 3 = Sun, Ascdt. or Descdt.
6. (a) (Ascdt. +Long. ofM.C) divided by 2; (b) (2 X Ascdt)
+ (2 X M.C.) divided by 3 = Sun or Moon.
This list can be multiplied by similar observations regard
ing the sensitive points. Thus Ascendant may replace Descen
dant, and M.C. may stand for I.C. Whatever we may do, does
the principle embodied here work satisfactorily? We are as
sured on good authority that the agreement comes true within
an orb of three degrees. This itself is enough to show that the
